Providers: (1) International House Madrid — €1,800. (2) British Council Madrid (Calle del General Martínez Campos 31) — €1,800. (3) Cambridge School Madrid — €1,700. DELTA (Diploma in English Language Teaching to Adults): Cambridge, advanced, for experienced teachers. 3 modules, can take over 1 year. €1,000-1,500 per module. Spanish as Foreign Language (ELE): For teaching Spanish. Instituto Cervantes DELE examiner course (€300-500). FP (Formación Profesional) in Madrid: vocational teacher training through Spanish vocational schools. ONLINE OPTIONS: (1) The TEFL Academy (€500, 168 hours). (2) Premier TEFL (€400-800). (3) International TEFL Academy (€1,200). Online TEFLs are accepted by some academies but the in-person practicum (teaching practice with real students) is what employers value. TIPS: (1) Choose a course with at least 6 hours of practicum. (2) International House and British Council are the most respected names. (3) Many courses offer job placement assistance. (4) TEFL alone is enough for academias; bilingual schools require a teaching license. (5) Online TEFL is cheaper but less competitive for top jobs.

title: Hostess & Promo Work (Azafata)
summary: Event hostess, promo, model work. €8-15/hour. Agencies: Model Management, Hostelería de España. Spanish required.
content: Hostess and promo work in Madrid. THE WORK: (1) Event hostess (azafata de eventos) — conferences, trade shows, product launches. Greeting guests, registration, info desk. €8-12/hour, usually 6-8 hour shifts. (2) Promo work (azafata de promoción) — sampling products in stores, street promotions, supermarket demos. €8-12/hour. (3) Model (modelo) — photo shoots, fashion shows, TV extras. €15-50/hour depending on job. (4) Brand ambassador — longer-term, representing a brand. €1,200-1,800/month. AGENCIES: (1) Model Management Madrid (Calle de la Ortega y Gasset 27) — modeling agency. (2) Five Stars (Calle de la Zorrilla 7) — hostess agency for trade shows (IFEMA events). (3) Hostelería de España (industry association) — connects with hotel/event work. (4) Tinto Events (Calle de la Cava Baja 32) — event staffing. (5) Atempo (Calle de la Príncipe de Vergara 32) — promo staffing. REQUIREMENTS: (1) Height: usually 1.65m+ for women, 1.75m+ for men (for fashion events). (2) Spanish: required for most work. English is a plus for international events. (3) Appearance: well-groomed, professional. (4) Photos: portfolio required (headshots + full body). (5) Availability: must be flexible for last-minute bookings. WHERE TO FIND: (1) Sign with 2-3 agencies (non-exclusive). (2) Facebook groups: 'Azafatas Madrid', 'Trabajo Azafata España'. (3) Infojobs.net — search 'azafata' or 'promotor'. (4) LinkedIn. TIPS: (1) Most agencies pay 30-60 days after the event — cash flow is an issue. (2) IFEMA events (fitur, Salón Inmobiliario, etc.) have the most work — January-June and September-November peak. (3) For non-Spanish speakers, focus on international events at IFEMA and hotels. (4) Be reliable — agencies blacklist no-shows. (5) The work is intermittent — combine with English teaching or other gig work for steady income.

title: Journalism & Writing Jobs in Madrid
summary: English-language journalism: El País English, The Local Spain, Marketwatch. Spanish required for most Spanish media.
content: Journalism and writing jobs in Madrid. ENGLISH-LANGUAGE MEDIA: (1) El País English edition (elpais.com/english) — Spain's largest newspaper, English section. Hires English-speaking journalists with Spanish fluency. Apply via LinkedIn or directly. (2) The Local Spain (thelocal.es) — English news site, hires English-speaking journalists. (3) EFE News Agency (efe.com) — Spanish news agency, has English service. (4) Marketwatch Madrid bureau — financial news, English. (5) Reuters Madrid — wires service, English. (6) AFP Madrid — French news agency, English service. (7) Madrid No Frills (madridnofrills.com) — independent English blog, takes pitches. (8) Devour Tours blog — food/travel content, English. SPANISH MEDIA (Spanish required): (1) El País, El Mundo, ABC, La Razón — national newspapers. (2) Marca, AS — sports dailies. (3) RTVE — public broadcaster. (4) Mediaset, Atresmedia — commercial TV. (5) Cadena SER, Onda Cero, COPE — radio. TRADE PRESS: (1) Cinco Días (business), Expansión (business), El Economista (business). (2) Hosteltur (tourism), OK Diario (politics). FREELANCE: (1) International media: pitch Spain-related stories to NYT, Guardian, BBC, etc. (2) Content marketing: many startups and agencies hire freelance writers. €0.10-0.30/word. (3) Translation: €0.06-0.12/word. (4) Copywriting: €30-80/hour. PROFESSIONAL ORGANIZATIONS: (1) Federación de Asociaciones de Periodistas de España (FAPE) — Spanish journalism association. (2) Madrid Foreign Press Association (Asociación de Prensa Extranjera) — for foreign correspondents. TIPS: (1) Spanish fluency is essential for most Spanish media jobs. (2) Build a portfolio — start a blog, pitch stories, get clips. (3) Networking: attend Madrid media events (Madrid Press Club, Espacio Fundación Telefónica). (4) Pay is low: €1,500-2,200/month for staff journalists. (5) Freelance is more flexible but unpredictable. (6) International correspondents usually need 5+ years experience and a posting from a home publication.

title: Cercanías Madrid (Commuter Rail)
summary: Madrid's commuter rail network. 9 lines (C1-C10). Connects city center to suburbs, airport, El Escorial, Alcalá. €1.70-5.60.
content: Cercanías Madrid is the commuter rail network operated by Renfe, serving Madrid and its metropolitan area. 9 lines (C1-C10) with 90+ stations. KEY LINES: (1) C1 — Atocha to Chamartín (8 min, faster than Metro), continues to Aeropuerto T4 (25 min) and Príncipe Pío. (2) C3 — Atocha to El Escorial (1 hour, €5.60). (3) C7 — Atocha to Alcalá de Henares (35 min, €2.60). (4) C8 — Atocha to Cercedilla (1h 15min, gateway to Sierra de Guadarrama). (5) C10 — Atocha to Chamartín to Madrid-Barajas T4. FARES: zone A (city center) €1.70-2.60; zone B1 (airport, closer suburbs) €2.60-3.40; zone B2 (El Escorial, Alcalá) €4.10-5.60. Tickets: buy at station machines (English option) or use a Tarjeta Transporte Público (10-trip card, 30% discount). Cercanías is FREE for holders of the Madrid City Card. FREQUENCY: every 5-15 min during peak, every 15-30 min off-peak. First train ~5am, last ~midnight. Less frequent on weekends. ADVANTAGES over Metro: (1) Faster for cross-city trips (Atocha to Chamartín in 8 min vs 15+ on Metro). (2) Goes to suburbs Metro doesn't reach (El Escorial, Alcalá). (3) Connects both major train stations to the airport. DISADVANTAGES: (1) Less frequent than Metro. (2) Delays during rush hour and signal failures. (3) Some older trains without AC. TIPS: Download the Renfe Cercanías app for real-time info. Follow @CercaniasMadrid on Twitter for delays. The C1 airport line is often faster than Metro L8.

title: Night Buses (Búhos) — Madrid 24h Transit
summary: Madrid's night bus network (búhos). 27 routes from Plaza de Cibeles. Run midnight-6am. €1.50-2.60. Frequents every 30-60 min.
content: When the Metro closes (1:30am Sun-Wed, 2:30am Fri-Sat), Madrid's night buses (búhos) take over. 27 routes (N1-N28), all starting from Plaza de Cibeles (the central hub). FARE: €1.50 single ticket, or use Tarjeta Transporte Público (€1.20 with the card). Routes fan out from Cibeles to all neighborhoods and suburbs. KEY ROUTES: (1) N1 — Cibeles to Sanchinarro (north). (2) N13 — Cibeles to Carabanchel (south). (3) N17 — Cibeles to Villaverde (south). (4) N4 — Cibeles to Barajas Airport (the only night transport to the airport, €1.50 — runs every 35 min). (5) N20 — Cibeles to Alcobendas (north). FREQUENCY: every 30 min on main routes (N1-N7), every 60 min on secondary routes. Last buses leave Cibeles around 5:30-6:00am (when Metro starts). TIPS: (1) All night buses pass through Plaza de Cibeles — use it as a transfer hub. (2) The N4 is the only public transport to Barajas Airport between 1:30am and 6am. (3) Buy tickets on the bus (cash) or use your Tarjeta Transporte Público. (4) Drivers don't always speak English — have your destination written down. (5) Buses can be crowded and rowdy on weekend nights (drunk passengers). (6) For women traveling alone at night, the front of the bus near the driver is safest. (7) The EMT Madrid app shows real-time night bus locations. ALTERNATIVE: Uber and Cabify operate 24/7, but surge pricing after 2am can be 2-3x normal rates. A taxi from the center to most neighborhoods is €10-15.

title: Coach Stations (Estaciones de Autobús)
summary: Madrid's 3 main coach stations: Estación Sur (long-distance), Moncloa (Castilla y León), Méndez Álvaro (south). Metro connected.
content: Madrid has 3 main coach (long-distance bus) stations, each serving different regions. ESTACIÓN SUR (also called Méndez Álvaro): Madrid's largest bus station. All ALSA long-distance routes depart here — Barcelona, Valencia, Andalusia, Portugal, France. 50+ platforms. Metro: Méndez Álvaro (L6). Open 24/7. Facilities: cafés, shops, ATMs, left luggage, taxi rank. Allow 20 min to find your platform. MONLOCA INTERCAMBIADOR: Serves Castilla y León (Segovia, Ávila, Salamanca, Zamora) and Galicia. Also serves some Sierra de Guadarrama routes. Metro: Moncloa (L3, L6). Modern interchange (2012), bright and clean. Open 6am-midnight. PLAZA ELÍPTICA: Smaller station serving southern routes — Toledo, Cuenca, Aranjuez, some Andalusia routes. Metro: Plaza Elíptica (L6, L11). Open 6am-11pm. AVANZA is the main operator from Moncloa and Plaza Elíptica; ALSA from Estación Sur. TIPS: (1) Arrive 20-30 min before departure to find your platform (large stations like Estación Sur are confusing). (2) Buy tickets online at alsa.es or avanzabus.es for the best fares. (3) Print or screenshot your ticket — some operators require printed tickets. (4) Luggage goes under the bus (free, 1 bag up to 30kg + 1 carry-on). (5) Coach stations are NOT the same as intercity bus stops — the stations have platforms, ticket windows, and facilities. (6) The Metro connection to all 3 stations is excellent — you can get from any Madrid neighborhood to any coach station in under 30 min.

title: Padel in Madrid
summary: Padel is Spain's #2 sport. 100+ clubs in Madrid. Top: Padel Club Madrid, Sangrano Padel. €20-40/court/hour. Easy to learn.
content: Padel is a racquet sport invented in Mexico, hugely popular in Spain (3 million players, second only to football). It's like squash-meets-tennis — played in doubles on an enclosed court with glass walls. The ball can bounce off the walls (like squash). Easier to learn than tennis — most beginners can rally after 30 min. Top clubs in Madrid: (1) Padel Club Madrid (Calle de la Calerilla 12, Las Tablas) — 14 courts, restaurant, pro shop. €30-40/court/hour peak, €20-25 off-peak. (2) Sangrano Padel Club (Calle del Santo Ángel de la Guarda 24, near Cuatro Caminos) — 9 courts, popular with expats. €25-35/hour. (3) Pádel La Finca (Pozuelo, west of Madrid) — 12 courts, upscale. €35-45/hour. (4) Centro Nacional de Pádel (Calle de Arroyo del Monte) — public, €20-25/hour. (5) Top Pádel Madrid Indoor (Calle del Payaso Fofó) — 8 indoor courts, €25-30/hour. BOOKING: most clubs use the Playtomic app (English) — find a partner, book a court, join games. FAMILIAR FACES: Real Madrid players, Spanish celebrities, and Spanish PM Pedro Sánchez all play padel. TIPS: (1) Wear padel-specific shoes (or tennis shoes with herringbone soles). (2) Racquets (palas) are different from tennis — shorter, solid, with holes. Rent €5 or buy €50-300. (3) Balls are similar to tennis but slightly smaller and lower pressure. (4) Scoring is identical to tennis. (5) After 2-3 lessons, you can play socially. (6) Madrid's climate allows outdoor padel year-round. (7) Padel is the best way to meet Spanish people — join the 'Madrid Pádel International' WhatsApp/Facebook groups.

title: Nightclubs & Electronic Music in Madrid
summary: Top clubs: Mondo, Fabrik, Capital, Goya Social Club. Tech house, techno, reggaeton. €15-30 entry. Open 1am-6am.
content: Madrid's club scene. TOP CLUBS: (1) Mondo Disko (Calle de la Alameda 28) — iconic, 30+ years, tech house and techno. €20-25 entry. Open Fri-Sat 1am-6am. (2) Fabrik (Calle de la Ctra. Fuenlabrada a Moraleja, Móstoles, 30 min south) — massive 5,000-capacity warehouse, EDM, techno. €25-30. Saturdays only. Free shuttle from Méndez Álvaro Metro. (3) Capital (Calle de la Gran Vía 8) — central, 2 rooms (house/techno + reggaeton/pop). €15-20. Open Thu-Sat midnight-6am. Popular with younger crowd. (4) Goya Social Club (Calle de Goya 43) — boutique, deep house, intimate (300 capacity). €15-20. (5) Spekummat (Calle de la Ballesta 14, Sol) — small, techno, underground. €10-15. (6) Room 18 (Hotel Ganivet, Plaza de España) — gay-friendly, mixed music. €15-20. (7) Pacha Madrid (Calle de la Castellana 27) — Ibiza brand, mixed crowd, EDM. €20-25. FESTIVALS: (1) Sonar Madrid (May) — electronic and avant-garde. (2) Mad Cool (July) — major music festival (indie, electronic, pop). €120-200 day ticket. (3) DCODE (September) — university-focused music festival. €40. (4) A Summer Story (June) — electronic. €60-80. TIPS: (1) Madrid clubs start late — don't arrive before 1:30am, peak is 3-4am. (2) Dress code: smart casual at most; no sportswear, no flip-flops. (3) Pre-game (botellón) in a park or apartment before going — drinks at clubs are €10-15. (4) Line up early for popular DJs (1am). (5) Use the app Discotech or wantickets.com to buy tickets in advance. (6) The Metro starts at 6am — perfect timing for the journey home. (7) Taxi/Uber after 3am can have 3x surge pricing.

title: Flamenco Tablaos (Live Flamenco Shows)
summary: Late-night flamenco shows at tablaos. Cardamomo, Corral de la Morería, Casa Patas. €30-50 with drink, €60-90 with dinner.
content: See our Flamenco Shows record (in Madrid Culture) for the full guide to flamenco tablaos in Madrid. This record focuses on the late-night (10pm+) shows that are part of Madrid's nightlife scene. LATE-NIGHT SHOWS: (1) Cardamomo (Calle de Echegaray 15) — 10pm and midnight shows, intimate, €30-35 with drink. The latest show (midnight) is the most authentic and rowdy. (2) Corral de la Morería (Calle de la Morería 17) — 10:30pm late show, €50-90 with dinner. The Michelin-starred restaurant makes this a dinner-and-show destination. (3) Casa Patas (Calle de Cañizares 10) — 10:30pm show, €35 with drink. After the show, the bar stays open with impromptu flamenco — sometimes better than the show itself. (4) Las Tablas (Plaza de España 9) — 10pm and midnight shows, €35. Modern tablaos with younger performers. (5) Torres Bermejas (Calle de la Flor Baja 9) — 10pm late show, €40-70. Larger venue. WHAT TO EXPECT AT LATE SHOWS: The midnight shows are where flamenco comes alive. Performers are loosened up, the audience is more engaged, and there's more improvisation. The atmosphere is smoky (despite smoking bans, some tablaos bend rules), intimate, and electric. TIPS: (1) Book the late show (midnight) for the most authentic experience. (2) Don't talk during the performances — Spaniards take flamenco seriously. (3) The 'with dinner' option is good but the food is secondary to the show. (4) Late shows end around 1:30-2am — perfect for continuing the night at a nearby bar. (5) Some tablaos have a bar after the show where performers and audience mingle — the best flamenco moments often happen here, spontaneously.

title: Jazz & Blues in Madrid
summary: Top jazz: Café Central, Clamores, Populart, Bogui Jazz. €10-20 entry + drink. Nightly shows from 9pm.
content: Madrid has a strong jazz scene. TOP JAZZ CLUBS: (1) Café Central (Plaza del Ángel 10, near Plaza Santa Ana) — Madrid's most famous jazz club, since 1982. 2 shows nightly (9pm, 11pm). €15-20 entry (includes drink). Open Tue-Sun. International and Spanish artists. Book ahead. (2) Clamores (Calle de Alburquerque 14, Malasaña) — historic, since 1981. Jam sessions, modern jazz. €10-12 entry. (3) Populart (Calle de las Huertas 22, Barrio de las Letras) — small, intimate, free entry (just buy drinks). Live jazz nightly 10pm-midnight. Best for casual listeners. (4) Bogui Jazz (Calle de la Barco 34, Malasaña) — newer, modern jazz, €10-15 entry. (5) Berlin Café (Calle de la Costanilla de San Andrés 20, La Latina) — café + jazz, free entry, drinks €6-10. (6) El Junco (Plaza de Santa Bárbara 10, Alonso Martínez) — jazz + blues + soul, free entry before 11pm, €5 after. FESTIVALS: (1) Madrid Jazz Festival (November-December) — international headliners at Circo Price, Teatro Fernán Gómez, Conde Duque. €20-60. (2) Jazz en la Latina (summer) — free outdoor jazz in La Latina's plazas. (3) Festival de Jazz de Madrid (Nov) — city-wide. BLUES: Madrid's blues scene is smaller. (1) Moe's Blues Bar (Calle de la Colegiata 9) — live blues Wed-Sat. €5-10. (2) Taberna El Almendro (Calle de la Almendro 13) — occasional blues. TIPS: (1) Café Central is the must-visit — book 1-2 days ahead. (2) Many jazz clubs have free entry early — arrive by 9pm. (3) Order a drink (€6-12) on top of the entry fee. (4) Spanish jazz audience is respectful — quiet during performances, applause between songs. (5) Madrid jazz festivals in November-December are world-class.

title: Official Madrid City Map (esMadrid)
summary: Free official Madrid city map from esMadrid. PDF + interactive. Tourist offices, airport, hotels.
content: The official Madrid city map is published by Madrid's tourism board (esMadrid). It's available in 3 formats: (1) PDF download (Spanish, English, French, German) at esmadrid.com/en/madrid-city-map — print before you go or save to your phone. (2) Interactive online map at esmadrid.com — searchable, with tourist attractions, Metro stations, and police stations marked. (3) Paper copies at any Tourist Office (Plaza Mayor, airport, Atocha) — free, high-quality print. The map covers central Madrid (inside the M-30 ring) in detail, with major sights, Metro lines, parks, and neighborhoods. A separate inset shows greater Madrid. TIPS: (1) The PDF is the best option — works offline, no data needed. (2) Print at A3 size if you can — easier to read. (3) The official map is more accurate than Google Maps for pedestrian zones and one-way streets. (4) For a Spanish-only but more detailed map, the Madrid city planning department publishes 'Plano de Madrid' at madrid.es. (5) The esMadrid app has the same map with GPS integration.

title: Madrid Metro Map (Official PDF)
summary: Official Metro de Madrid map. PDF download + printed at every station. 12 lines, 300+ stations.
content: The official Madrid Metro map is published by Metro de Madrid. Formats: (1) PDF download at metromadrid.es/en (English) — print before you go or save to phone. (2) Free printed map at any Metro station ticket office (ask 'un plano del Metro, por favor'). (3) Interactive map in the Metro de Madrid app (English) with route planner. (4) Wall maps at every station entrance and platform. The map shows all 12 Metro lines (color-coded), 300+ stations, interchange connections (Renfe Cercanías, EMT buses, tram), and zone boundaries (A, B1, B2, B3). Key features: (1) Lines are color-coded (L1 light blue, L2 red, L3 yellow, L4 brown, L5 green, L6 grey/circular, L7 orange, L8 pink/airport, L9 purple, L10 dark blue, L11 green, L12 light green/Metrosur). (2) Interchange stations are marked with a special icon. (3) The map includes the Renfe Cercanías network overlay. (4) Airport stations (Barajas T1-T2-T3, T4) are clearly marked. TIPS: (1) Download the PDF and save offline — no data needed. (2) The Metro app route planner is excellent — faster than Google Maps for Metro-only routes. (3) Free paper maps at stations are great backups. (4) The map is updated 2-3 times per year — check for the latest version.

title: Cercanías Madrid Map (Commuter Rail)
summary: Official Cercanías Madrid map. 9 lines (C1-C10) covering Madrid + suburbs. PDF + app.
content: The Cercanías Madrid map shows the commuter rail network. Formats: (1) PDF download at renfe.com/en (English) — search 'Cercanías Madrid plano'. (2) Free printed map at Atocha and Chamartín stations (ticket office). (3) Interactive map in the Renfe Cercanías app (Spanish only). (4) Wall maps at every Cercanías station. The map shows all 9 lines (C1-C10) and 90+ stations, covering Madrid and its metropolitan area. Key features: (1) Lines are color-coded (C1 blue, C2 green, C3 purple, C4 red, C5 orange, C7 grey, C8 dark green, C9 brown, C10 light blue). (2) Major interchanges with Metro are marked. (3) Zone boundaries (A, B1, B2, C1, C2) are shown — important for fares. (4) Airport connection (C1 to Barajas T4) is highlighted. (5) Connections to other Cercanías networks (Guadalajara, Segovia) are shown. TIPS: (1) Cercanías is faster than Metro for cross-city trips (Atocha to Chamartín in 8 min). (2) The C1 line is the most useful for tourists (Atocha, Chamartín, Airport T4, Príncipe Pío). (3) Download the PDF and save offline. (4) The Renfe Cercanías app has a route planner (Spanish only — use Google Translate). (5) Real-time train info at @CercaniasMadrid on Twitter.

title: EMT Bus Map & Route Planner
summary: EMT Madrid bus network map + route planner app. 200+ lines. Real-time arrivals in the app.
content: EMT (Empresa Municipal de Transportes) operates Madrid's city buses. The network is too complex for a single map, so the official resources are: (1) EMT Madrid app (English) — the best resource. Includes route planner, real-time bus arrivals, saved routes, ticket purchase. Free. (2) Interactive map at emtmadrid.es/English — shows all 200+ lines, searchable by line number or stop. (3) Individual line maps (PDF) at emtmadrid.es — search for a specific line number. (4) Printed line maps at major bus stops (sometimes). KEY BUS LINES for tourists: (1) 1, 2, 46 — north-south through the center. (2) 27, 45, 150 — to museums (Prado, Reina Sofía). (3) 74 — to El Rastro flea market. (4) 133, 134 — to Las Tablas business district. (5) 200, 203 — airport express buses. NIGHT BUSES (Búhos): 27 routes from Cibeles, N1-N28. The EMT app shows real-time locations of all buses on a map. TIPS: (1) Download the EMT Madrid app — it's essential for bus travel. (2) Bus stops have QR codes that show real-time arrivals (scan with phone). (3) The app's route planner integrates with Metro and Cercanías. (4) Day passes (€5.20) include Metro, EMT buses, and Cercanías zone A. (5) Most bus stops have shelters with route maps posted.

title: Madrid Cycling Maps (BiciMAD + Bike Lanes)
summary: Madrid bike lane maps + BiciMAD station map. 130km of bike lanes. Anillo Verde Ciclista 65km loop.
content: Madrid cycling maps. BIKE LANE MAP: Madrid has 130km of dedicated bike lanes, with more being added yearly. The official map (madrid.es, Spanish only) shows all bike lanes, cycle paths, and bike-friendly streets. Print version free at bicycle shops and tourist offices. BICIMAD STATION MAP: 250+ electric bike stations across Madrid. Live availability shown in the BiciMAD app (English) and at bicimad.com. Search by address or use the map. ANILLO VERDE CICLISTA: The 65km 'Green Cycling Ring' is Madrid's longest continuous bike path, circling the city through parks and green corridors. The official map shows the full route with access points. PDF at madrid.es. MADRID RÍO BIKE PATH: 10km linear park along the Manzanares River, dedicated bike path, connects to Casa de Campo. CASA DE CAMPO TRAILS: 1,535 hectares of trails, mostly unpaved, popular with mountain bikers. No official map — use Trailforks or MTB project apps. RETIRO PARK: 4.5km perimeter loop, flat, bike-friendly (but crowded on weekends). BEST APPS: (1) BiciMAD (English) — for the electric bike-share. (2) Komoot (English, paid) — route planning. (3) Strava (English, free) — heatmaps show popular routes. (4) Google Maps — bike mode shows bike lanes (but not always accurate). TIPS: (1) Cycling in central Madrid is possible but tricky — narrow streets, aggressive drivers. (2) Best cycling is in parks (Retiro, Casa de Campo, Madrid Río) and on the Anillo Verde. (3) Helmets mandatory on intercity roads, recommended in city. (4) Bikes on Metro: allowed outside rush hours (no e-bikes on Metro).

title: Madrid Walking Maps & Self-Guided Routes
summary: 7 official self-guided walking routes from esMadrid. Habsburg, Bourbon, Literary, Civil War, etc. PDF + audio.
content: Madrid walking maps and self-guided routes. OFFICIAL WALKING ROUTES (esMadrid): 7 official routes, each with PDF map + audio guide (English). Free download at esmadrid.com/en/audio-guides. (1) Habsburg Madrid (2h) — 16th-17th century old town. Starts Plaza Mayor. (2) Bourbon Madrid (1.5h) — 18th century. Starts Puerta de Alcalá. (3) Literary Quarter (1h) — Barrio de las Letras. Starts Plaza de Santa Ana. (4) Madrid of the Austrias (2.5h) — Royal Palace area. (5) Modernist Madrid (1.5h) — Gran Vía and Salamanca. (6) Parks and Gardens (2h) — Retiro and Madrid Río. (7) Civil War Madrid (2h) — historical sites. PRINT MAPS: Tourist offices (Plaza Mayor, airport, Atocha) give out free walking route maps. INTERACTIVE: Google Maps has walking directions and works offline (download Madrid map area first). Citymapper (free app) has good walking directions with estimated times. NEIGHBORHOOD MAPS: Each of our 10 neighborhood records (see/neighborhoods) has a suggested walking route in its content. SPECIALTY WALKING MAPS: (1) Madrid No Frills (madridnofrills.com) — independent walking routes. (2) Madrid Foodie — tapas crawl maps. (3) Madrid Urban Sketchers — sketching routes. TIPS: (1) Madrid is very walkable — most sights within 20-30 min walk of Puerta del Sol. (2) Avoid midday in summer (1-5pm) — too hot. (3) Wear comfortable shoes — Madrid has lots of cobblestones. (4) Carry water — public fountains are rare in central Madrid. (5) Print the PDF maps — phone batteries die. (6) The esMadrid audio guides are excellent — download to phone before you go.

title: Interactive Madrid Maps (Google, Citymapper, esMadrid)
summary: Best interactive Madrid maps: Google Maps (download offline), Citymapper (transit), esMadrid (tourist).
content: Interactive Madrid maps. GOOGLE MAPS: The default choice. (1) Download Madrid offline map (search 'Madrid' → Download) — about 200MB. Works without data. (2) Walking, driving, transit, and bike directions. (3) Real-time traffic. (4) Street View for previewing your destination. (5) Reviews of restaurants, hotels, attractions. TIPS: Google Maps is generally accurate in Madrid, but sometimes gets pedestrian zones wrong in the old town. CITYMAPPER (free app, English): Better than Google for transit. (1) Real-time Metro, bus, Cercanías arrivals. (2) Compares all transit options (fastest, cheapest, fewest transfers). (3) Shows where to stand on the Metro for fastest exit. (4) Includes bike-share (BiciMAD) and ride-sharing (Uber, Cabify, taxi). (5) Offline mode for routes you've searched before. ESMADRÍ APP (free, English): Madrid's official tourism app. (1) Tourist-focused map with attractions, restaurants, hotels. (2) Self-guided walking routes (7 official routes). (3) Audio guides. (4) Event listings. (5) Tourist office locations. MADRID MOBILITY APP (free, Spanish): City's official transit app. (1) All transit (Metro, EMT, Cercanías). (2) Real-time arrivals. (3) BiciMAD station availability. (4) Parking availability. (5) Buy tickets. SPECIALTY MAPS: (1) AllTrails (paid) — hiking trails near Madrid (Sierra de Guadarrama). (2) Komoot (paid) — cycling routes. (3) Trailforks (free) — mountain biking trails. (4) Park4Night (free) — camper parking. (5) Flush (free) — public toilets. TIPS: (1) Download Google Maps offline before you arrive — saves data and battery. (2) Citymapper is the best for transit in Madrid. (3) The esMadrid app is worth downloading for the walking routes and audio guides. (4) For hiking in the Sierra, AllTrails or Komoot are worth the paid subscription.

title: Neighborhood Maps (Barrio Guides)
summary: 10 official neighborhood maps from esMadrid. Each shows top sights, restaurants, Metro stations for that barrio.
content: Madrid neighborhood maps. esMadrid publishes free neighborhood (barrio) guides with maps for 10 of Madrid's main districts. Each guide includes: (1) A printable PDF map showing top sights, restaurants, shops, and Metro stations. (2) A brief history and character description. (3) Recommended walking routes. (4) Top 5 things to see. (5) Top 5 places to eat. Available at esmadrid.com/en/neighborhoods (English). NEIGHBORHOODS WITH OFFICIAL GUIDES: (1) Sol & Gran Vía — tourist center. (2) Malasaña — hipster/alternative. (3) Chueca — LGBTQ+ and chic. (4) La Latina — tapas and traditional. (5) Lavapiés — multicultural. (6) Chamberí — local residential. (7) Salamanca — upscale luxury. (8) Retiro — parks and museums. (9) Argüelles & Moncloa — students and parks. (10) Barrio de las Letras — literary quarter. PRINTED COPIES: Free at any Tourist Office (Plaza Mayor, airport, Atocha). Each guide is 8-12 pages, A5 size. ALSO USEFUL: (1) Each of our 10 neighborhood records (see/neighborhoods) has a sub-subcategory (City Center, Historic, Residential, Upscale, Student & West, North & Diverse). (2) Madrid Secreto (madridsecreto.co, Spanish) has alternative neighborhood guides. (3) Madrid No Frills (madridnofrills.com, English) has authentic neighborhood walking routes. TIPS: (1) Pick up the printed neighborhood guides at the Plaza Mayor tourist office on your first day. (2) The esMadrid neighborhood maps are excellent — they show pedestrian zones that Google Maps sometimes misses. (3) For each neighborhood, plan 2-4 hours of walking. (4) Combine 2-3 adjacent neighborhoods in a day: Sol + La Latina, Malasaña + Chueca, Retiro + Jerónimos.

title: Left Luggage at Barajas Airport
summary: Left luggage at Madrid-Barajas Airport. T1 and T4. €4-5.50/bag/day. Open 24/7.
content: Barajas Airport has left luggage (consignas) at two terminals. T1 (Terminal 1): located in the arrivals area, near parking P1. €4/bag/day (any size). Open 24/7. T4 (Terminal 4): located in the arrivals area, level 0. €5.50/bag/day. Open 24/7. T2 and T3 do NOT have left luggage — walk to T1 (5 min covered walkway). PRICES: T1 €4/bag/day, T4 €5.50/bag/day. Pay by cash or card. Maximum bag size: 80cm x 50cm x 30cm. SECURITY: bags are X-rayed before storage. ID required (passport). Staffed 24/7 with CCTV. TIPS: (1) The airport is the most expensive left luggage in Madrid — only use it if you have a layover. (2) For sightseeing before a flight, consider left luggage at Atocha (€3.90/day) instead — cheaper and closer to the center. (3) Allow 15-20 min for drop-off and pickup during peak hours. (4) Don't leave electronics, documents, or valuables. (5) Take a photo of your bag and keep the claim ticket. See also our master Left Luggage record and the Barajas Airport record.

title: Left Luggage at Atocha Station
summary: Left luggage at Atocha train station. €3.90-5.50/bag/day. Open 5:30am-10:30pm. Near the tropical garden.
content: Atocha Station has the most convenient left luggage in central Madrid. LOCATION: ground floor, near the tropical garden (the old station hall with 4,000 plants). Follow signs for 'Consignas'. PRICES: €3.90/bag/day (size S/M, up to 60cm), €5.50/bag/day (size L, over 60cm). Pay by cash or card. HOURS: 5:30am-10:30pm daily (last drop-off 10pm, last pickup 10:30pm). SECURITY: bags are X-rayed. ID required (passport or NIE). Staffed with CCTV. MAXIMUM STAY: 30 days (then bags are sent to lost property). TIPS: (1) Atocha is the best option for sightseeing — central location, near the Prado and Reina Sofía. (2) Arrive early (before 9am) to avoid lines — cruise ship groups and tour buses use this facility. (3) The tropical garden is free to visit while you wait. (4) Don't leave valuables or electronics. (5) Keep your claim ticket — losing it requires ID verification and a 30+ min wait. (6) For longer storage, see Public Storage (Trasteros). (7) Atocha also has a VIP luggage service (left luggage + delivery to your hotel) for €15-25.

title: Left Luggage at Chamartín Station
summary: Left luggage at Chamartín train station. €3.90-5.50/bag/day. Open 6am-11pm. Near the VIPS café.
content: Chamartín Station (Madrid's second major train station, north of center) has left luggage facilities. LOCATION: ground floor, near the VIPS café. Follow signs for 'Consignas'. PRICES: same as Atocha — €3.90/bag/day (size S/M, up to 60cm), €5.50/bag/day (size L, over 60cm). HOURS: 6am-11pm daily (last drop-off 10:30pm, last pickup 11pm). SECURITY: bags are X-rayed. ID required (passport or NIE). Staffed with CCTV. MAXIMUM STAY: 30 days. TIPS: (1) Chamartín is less crowded than Atocha — usually no lines. (2) Chamartín is in the north of Madrid — use it if you're staying in Chamartín, Tetuán, or financial district. (3) For tourists staying in the center, Atocha is more convenient. (4) The station has good food options (VIPS, Starbucks) while you wait. (5) Don't leave valuables or electronics. (6) Keep your claim ticket. (7) Chamartín connects to Barajas Airport via Cercanías C1 (25 min) — if you have a layover, you could store bags at Chamartín and take the train to the airport.

title: Left Luggage at Bus Stations (Estación Sur)
summary: Left luggage at Estación Sur (Méndez Álvaro) bus station. €3/bag/day. Open 6am-midnight.
content: Estación Sur (also called Méndez Álvaro) — Madrid's main long-distance bus station — has left luggage. LOCATION: inside the station, ground floor, near platform 1. Follow signs for 'Consignas'. PRICES: €3/bag/day (any size). Cheapest left luggage in Madrid. HOURS: 6am-midnight daily (last drop-off 11:30pm). SECURITY: bags are X-rayed. ID required. Staffed with CCTV. TIPS: (1) Estación Sur is the cheapest option (€3 vs €3.90-5.50 at train stations). (2) Located south of center — take Metro L6 to Méndez Álvaro (15 min from Sol). (3) Less crowded than Atocha. (4) The bus station has food courts and shops while you wait. (5) Other bus stations (Moncloa, Plaza Elíptica) do NOT have left luggage — only Estación Sur. (6) Don't leave valuables. (7) Keep your claim ticket. ALTERNATIVE: If you're near Moncloa or Plaza Elíptica, consider taking the Metro to Estación Sur specifically for the cheaper luggage storage.

title: Public Storage (Trasteros) — Long-Term
summary: Self-storage units (trasteros) for long-term storage. €50-150/month. Chains: Accessa, City Trastero, Trastero Madrid.
content: Trasteros are self-storage units — like Public Storage in the US or Access Self Storage in the UK. Used for long-term storage (weeks, months, or years) — much cheaper than left luggage for extended periods. MAIN CHAINS: (1) Accessa (accessa.es) — Spain's largest, 8 locations in Madrid area. Units from 1m² to 20m². €50-200/month depending on size. 24/7 access at most locations. (2) City Trastero (citytrastero.com) — 4 locations in Madrid. €60-150/month. Modern, clean. (3) Trastero Madrid (trasteromadrid.com) — local, 3 locations. €45-120/month. (4) Storage Madrid — small, central. (5) Ibertrastero — chain, multiple. PRICING: 1m² (a few boxes) €45-60/month. 3m² (small apartment contents) €80-120/month. 6m² (1-bedroom apartment) €120-180/month. 10m²+ (house) €180-300/month. First month often discounted (€1 promotions). WHAT'S INCLUDED: 24/7 access (most), CCTV, climate control (some), free truck + driver for move-in (some), packing supplies for sale. USES: (1) Between apartments (store furniture during a move). (2) For expats going home for summer. (3) For businesses storing inventory. (4) For students storing stuff between academic years. TIPS: (1) Compare prices — there's a big range. (2) Negotiate — ask for a discount, especially for 6+ month commitments. (3) Check access hours — some are 24/7, some 8am-8pm. (4) Insurance: usually €10-15/month extra. (5) Climate control matters for electronics, art, leather — ask if the unit has it. (6) Reserve 1-2 weeks ahead in summer (peak season for student storage).

title: Useful Information (Practical Guide)
summary: Practical info for visitors: electricity, water, taxes, opening hours, public holidays, language, currency.
content: Practical information for visitors to Madrid. ELECTRICITY: 230V, 50Hz. Plug type C/F (two round pins). US/UK visitors need adapters. Spanish sockets accept Type C and Type F plugs. WATER: Madrid tap water is safe to drink and excellent quality (from mountain reservoirs). Bottled water is unnecessary. CURRENCY: Euro (€). ATMs everywhere. Credit/debit cards accepted almost everywhere (Visa, Mastercard). American Express less common. Contactless payment (Apple Pay, Google Pay) widely accepted. TAXES: VAT (IVA) is 21% on most goods and services, 10% on restaurants and hotels, 4% on basic food. All prices include IVA — what you see is what you pay. OPENING HOURS: Shops Mon-Sat 10am-10pm (some close 2-5pm). Restaurants lunch 1-4pm, dinner 8:30pm-midnight. Banks Mon-Fri 8:30am-2:30pm. Museums Tue-Sun 10am-8pm (most closed Mondays). PUBLIC HOLIDAYS (when most things close): Jan 1 (New Year), Jan 6 (Three Kings), May 1 (Labor Day), May 2 (Madrid Day), May 15 (San Isidro), Aug 15 (Assumption), Oct 12 (Hispanic Day), Nov 1 (All Saints), Dec 6 (Constitution), Dec 8 (Immaculate Conception), Dec 25 (Christmas). LANGUAGE: Spanish (Castilian). English spoken at major hotels, tourist sights, and younger people. Madrid has co-official languages in surrounding regions (Catalan in Catalonia, Galician in Galicia, Basque in Basque Country). TIME ZONE: Central European Time (CET, UTC+1) in winter, Central European Summer Time (CEST, UTC+2) in summer. EMERGENCY: 112 (free, multilingual). PHARMACIES: Look for green cross. 24-hour pharmacies rotate — search 'farmacia de guardia Madrid'. TIPPING: Optional. Restaurants 5-10%, taxis round up. SMOKING: Banned in all indoor public spaces, including bars and restaurants. E-cigarettes included. ALCOHOL: Legal age 18. Drinking in public (streets, parks) is illegal except in designated areas during festivals. DRIVING: Drive on the right. Speed limits: 50 km/h city, 90 km/h highway, 120 km/h motorway. Seatbelts mandatory. Blood alcohol limit 0.5g/L (stricter than US/UK).

title: Spanish Culture — A Quick Guide
summary: Spanish customs: mealtimes, siesta (mostly gone), personal space, greetings, holidays, family life.
content: Spanish culture for visitors. MEALTIMES (later than northern Europe): Breakfast (desayuno) 7-9am (coffee + toast/pastry). Mid-morning snack (almuerzo) 11am-noon (bocadillo). Lunch (comida) 2-3:30pm (main meal, often 3 courses). Afternoon snack (merienda) 6-7pm (small). Dinner (cena) 9-11pm (lighter than lunch). Restaurants don't serve lunch before 1pm or dinner before 8:30pm. SIESTA: The traditional afternoon nap is mostly gone in Madrid (especially for office workers). Some shops still close 2-5pm in smaller towns, but in Madrid most shops stay open. Spanish people do stay up later than northern Europeans — dinner at 10pm is normal. GREETINGS: (1) Handshake for business/first meetings. (2) Two kisses (one on each cheek, starting with the right) for friends and social situations. (3) 'Hola, buenas' is a casual greeting usable anytime. (4) 'Buenos días' (morning), 'Buenas tardes' (afternoon), 'Buenas noches' (evening/night). PERSONAL SPACE: Spaniards stand closer when talking and touch more (arm on shoulder, etc.) than northern Europeans. This is normal, not flirtatious. FAMILY: Family is central to Spanish life. Many young adults live with parents until 30+. Sunday family lunch is sacred. Multigenerational households are common. TIME: Spaniards have a relaxed attitude toward time. Being 10-15 min late to a social meeting is normal. Business meetings start on time. QUEUING: Spaniards don't queue as formally as the British — it's more of a crowd. Don't be too polite about waiting your turn — you'll never get served. FOOTBALL: Real Madrid vs Atlético is a serious divide. Don't wear a Real Madrid shirt in Atlético areas and vice versa. BULLFIGHTING: Controversial. Some Spaniards love it, others consider it animal cruelty. Catalonia and Canary Islands have banned it. RELIGION: Spain is officially Catholic, but most Spaniards are non-practicing. Easter (Semana Santa) processions are cultural events even for atheists. TIPPING: Optional, 5-10% in restaurants. FLAMENCO: From Andalusia, not Madrid — but Madrid has the best flamenco tablaos. SPANISH HUMOR: Direct, loud, physical, often political. Different from British irony or American sarcasm.

title: Bus vs Coach Stations in Madrid
summary: Madrid has both city bus stops (EMT) and coach stations (intercity). This explains the difference.
content: Madrid has two different types of bus infrastructure — city buses (EMT) and intercity coaches (ALSA, Avanza). CITY BUSES (EMT): Operated by Empresa Municipal de Transportes. 200+ lines within Madrid city. Stops every 200-400m on major streets. Marked with red/white signs showing line numbers. €1.50 single ticket (€1.20 with Tarjeta Transporte Público). No stations — just stops. See our EMT Buses record. COACH STATIONS (intercity/long-distance): Three main stations for buses leaving Madrid — Estación Sur (Méndez Álvaro) for ALSA long-distance, Moncloa for Avanza (Castilla y León, Galicia), Plaza Elíptica for southern routes. See our Coach Stations record. KEY DIFFERENCE: City buses (EMT) operate within Madrid city limits, stop every few blocks, and use simple stops. Coaches (autocares) travel between cities, depart from stations with platforms and ticket offices, and require advance booking. CONFUSION: The Spanish word 'autobús' is used for BOTH city buses and intercity coaches. Context matters. 'Autobús' or 'bus' usually means city bus. 'Autocar' or 'coach' means long-distance. TIPS: (1) For getting around Madrid: use EMT buses (see our EMT Buses record). (2) For travel to other cities: use ALSA or Avanza coaches from one of the 3 coach stations. (3) The Metro is usually faster than EMT buses for tourists — use buses only when Metro doesn't go where you need. (4) For coach travel, book ahead at alsa.es or avanzabus.es. (5) Bus stops in Madrid are well-marked with route maps and real-time arrival displays (or QR codes to scan with your phone).

title: Taxi Companies & Booking Apps
summary: Madrid taxi booking apps: PideTaxi, FreeNow, Radio Taxi. White taxis with red stripe. €30 flat airport fare.
content: Madrid taxis are white with a red diagonal stripe and the Madrid bear (oso y el madroño) crest on the doors. BOOKING APPS: (1) PideTaxi (pidetaxi.com) — official Madrid taxi app. English available. Same fares as street hail (no surge pricing). Free to use. (2) FreeNow (formerly MyTaxi) — international taxi app. English. Same fares. Slightly higher demand than PideTaxi. (3) Radio Taxi Independiente (91 447 51 84) — phone dispatch, Spanish only. (4) Radio Teléfono Taxi (91 547 82 00) — phone dispatch, Spanish only. (5) Teletaxi (91 445 90 08) — phone dispatch. WHEN TO USE APPS vs STREET HAIL: (1) Use apps at night, in the rain, or in areas with few taxis. (2) Street hail is faster in central Madrid during the day. (3) Always use the official apps — avoid unlicensed 'taxis' that solicit at tourist spots. FARES: (1) Initial fare €2.40 (daytime). (2) €1.10/km city. (3) €1.30/km outside city or at night. (4) Airport supplement €5.50. (5) Train/bus station supplement €2.95. (6) Waiting time €22/hour. TYPICAL FARES: Sol to Retiro €8-10. Sol to Bernabéu €12-15. Center to airport €30 (flat rate inside M-30). Center to IFEMA €25-30. TIPS: (1) Madrid taxis are honest — scams are rare. (2) Always use the meter (taxímetro). Refuse if the driver wants a fixed price (except airport flat rate). (3) Tip: round up to the nearest euro, or €1-2 for good service. (4) Taxis can use bus lanes — faster than driving. (5) 4 passengers max per taxi. (6) Large luggage goes in the trunk (free). (7) For airport runs, the €30 flat rate (inside M-30) is usually cheaper than the meter.

title: Airport Shuttle Services (Door-to-Door)
summary: Shared shuttle vans from Barajas to your hotel. €10-20/person. Companies: Aerocity, Air Shuttle Madrid.
content: Shared airport shuttles are a middle ground between the €5 express bus and the €30 taxi. You share a van with 4-8 other passengers, dropping each at their hotel. COMPANIES: (1) Aerocity (aerocity.com) — Madrid's main shared shuttle. €16.50/person one-way to central Madrid hotels, €27 round-trip. English website. Book online 12+ hours ahead. Door-to-door service. (2) Air Shuttle Madrid — €15/person. Book at the airport (T1, T4 arrivals) or online. (3) Suntransfers Shared — €12-18/person. International booking platform. (4) Welcome Pickups Shared — €14/person. PRICING: €10-20 per person one-way, depending on company and destination zone. Round-trip discounts available. Cheaper than a taxi for solo travelers, more expensive for groups of 3+. HOW IT WORKS: (1) Book online 12-48 hours ahead. (2) On arrival, call the company or go to their meeting point (sign with your name). (3) Wait 10-20 min for other passengers. (4) Van drops you at your hotel (allow 45-90 min total depending on other drops). PROS: Door-to-door, cheaper than taxi for solo travelers, English-speaking drivers. CONS: Slower than taxi (other stops), must book ahead, limited luggage space (1-2 bags per person). TIPS: (1) Book ahead — walk-up shuttles are rare. (2) For 3+ people, a taxi (€30 flat rate) is cheaper and faster. (3) For solo travelers on a budget, the Airport Express Bus (€5) is cheaper but doesn't go to your hotel. (4) Shuttles run 24/7 but late-night service is less frequent. (5) Have your hotel address and phone number ready for the driver.

title: Optical Shops (Ópticas) in Madrid
summary: Optical shops: Óptica Universitaria, Alain Afflelou, Multiópticas. Eye exams €40-80, glasses €80-300.
content: Optical shops (ópticas) in Madrid. MAIN CHAINS: (1) Alain Afflelou — French chain, 50+ Madrid locations, eye exams €40-60, glasses from €80. (2) Multiópticas — Spanish chain, similar pricing. (3) Óptica Universitaria (Calle Gran Vía 28) — central, popular with students. (4) Cottet — Catalan chain, premium. (5) Óptica Suiza — high-end. INDEPENDENT: (1) Óptica Besada (Calle de la Velázquez 21) — English-speaking, premium. (2) Óptica Val (Calle de la Alcalá 55) — mid-range. SERVICES: Eye exams (examen de la vista) €40-80, sometimes free with glasses purchase. Contact lens fitting €50-80. Glasses (gafas) from €80 (basic) to €300+ (designer). Contact lenses (lentillas) €30-60/month. Sunglasses (gafas de sol) from €50. INSURANCE: Spanish public health covers eye exams for under-18s and over-65s. Private insurance (Sanitas, Adeslas) often includes optical coverage €100-200/year. TIPS: (1) Bring your prescription — Spanish opticians accept international prescriptions. (2) Glasses are ready in 1-2 hours for standard lenses, 1-2 weeks for special prescriptions. (3) Tax-free shopping available for non-EU residents on purchases over €90. (4) Bargaining is not common but chains sometimes offer promotions (2-for-1, free eye exam with purchase).

title: Music Stores in Madrid
summary: Music stores: El Corte Inglés music, Discos Melopelo (vinyl), Mariscal (instruments). CDs €10-20, vinyl €15-30.
content: Music stores in Madrid. CD/DVD: (1) El Corte Inglés (multiple) — Spain's largest CD/DVD selection, 7th floor of Castellana store. Mainstream and Spanish artists. CDs €10-18, DVDs €10-20. (2) FNAC (Calle Marqués de Casa Riera 4) — good selection, similar pricing. VINYL: (1) Discos Melopelo (Calle de la Colegiata 12, La Latina) — Madrid's best vinyl shop, new and used, €15-30 per LP. Jazz, rock, flamenco, electronic. (2) Flash Records (Calle de la Escorial 6, Malasaña) — used vinyl, €10-25. (3) Bicolor Music (Calle de la Pez 8) — new vinyl, focus on indie. (4) Disco 100 (Calle de la Montera 30) — dance/electronic vinyl. INSTRUMENTS: (1) Mariscal (Calle de la Marqués de Cubas 4) — Madrid's oldest music shop (1921), guitars, violins, brass. (2) Casa Luthier (Calle de la Balmes 22) — classical guitars, high-end. (3) Yamaha Music Madrid (Calle de la Alcalá 119) — pianos, keyboards, all instruments. (4) Guitar Center Madrid (Calle de la Atocha 28) — guitars, amps. CONCERT TICKETS: (1) El Corte Inglés ticket counters — large events. (2) Tick Tack Ticket (online) — most concerts. (3) Wegow (online) — indie concerts. TIPS: (1) Vinyl is having a renaissance in Madrid — Melopelo is the must-visit. (2) Spanish music to look for: flamenco (Paco de Lucía, Camarón), pop (Alaska, Rosalía), rock (Héroes del Silencio, Jarabe de Palo). (3) El Corte Inglés has the best selection of Spanish classical and flamenco CDs.

title: International Grocery Stores
summary: International food: Mantequerías Alemanas (German), Tatsumi (Japanese), Seoul (Korean), Lavapiés (Indian/African).
content: International grocery stores in Madrid for expat and specialty foods. BRITISH/IRISH: (1) The Irish Shop (Calle de la Espíritu Santo 1, Malasaña) — HP sauce, Marmite, Cadbury, Walker's crisps. (2) UK food sections at El Corte Inglés (Castellana, Goya) — PG Tips, McVitie's, Colman's. GERMAN: (1) Mantequerías Alemanas (Calle de Ayala 25, Salamanca) — German bread, sausages, beer, mustard. Established 1930. JAPANESE: (1) Tatsumi (Calle del General Yagüe 14) — Japanese groceries, sushi-grade fish, miso, nori, sake. (2) Warehouse Japanese Market (Calle de la Orense 31) — larger selection. KOREAN: (1) Seoul (Calle de la Princesa 5) — kimchi, gochujang, Korean snacks. (2) Korean Market (Calle de la Luna 3) — smaller. CHINESE: (1) Casa Asia (Calle del Cardenal Cisneros 9) — has a small Asian food section. (2) Barrio de Usera (south Madrid) — Madrid's Chinatown, 20+ Chinese supermarkets. INDIAN/PAKISTANI: (1) Lavapiés neighborhood (Calle de Lavapiés, Calle de la Ollerías) — 10+ Indian grocery stores, spices, lentils, basmati. (2) Yatai Market (Calle de la Lavapiés 28) — pan-Asian. LATIN AMERICAN: (1) Mercado de la Cebada (La Latina) — Latin American products. (2) Calle de la Tolmo (Lavapiés) — Peruvian, Colombian, Ecuadorian. AFRICAN: (1) Calle de la Lavapiés (south end) — Senegalese, Nigerian products. HALAL: (1) Halal Butcher shops in Lavapiés and Cuatro Caminos. ITALIAN: (1) Obika (various) — mozzarella bar, Italian products. (2) Giglio (Calle de la Jorge Juan 10) — Italian deli. AMERICAN: (1) Taste of America (Calle de la Modesto Lafuente 45) — American candy, baking supplies, Pepsi Wild Cherry. TIPS: (1) El Corte Inglés Gourmet section (Castellana) has the best all-in-one international selection. (2) For Asian ingredients, Usera is cheapest. (3) For Latin American, La Latina is best. (4) Most international stores are closed Sundays.

title: Architects & General Contractors (Refurbishment)
summary: Architects and contractors for apartment renovation. Permits required from City Hall. Budget €800-1,500/m² for full renovation.
content: Architects and contractors for apartment renovation in Madrid. WHEN YOU NEED AN ARCHITECT: (1) Structural changes (removing walls, changing layout). (2) Changing facade or windows. (3) Adding bathrooms or kitchens. (4) Any work requiring a city permit (licencia de obra). WHEN YOU NEED A CONTRACTOR (without architect): (1) Painting, flooring. (2) Kitchen/bathroom replacement (no layout change). (3) Electrical and plumbing upgrades. (4) Minor repairs. ARCHITECTS: (1) COAM (Colegio Oficial de Arquitectos de Madrid) — the official architects' association. Search their directory at coam.org. Rates: €80-150/hour or 8-12% of project cost. (2) Recommended English-speaking architects: BmasC Arquitectos, Studio Marco Vermeulen, OOAA Arquitectura. CONTRACTORS (Empresas de Reformas): (1) Reformas Madrid (multiple) — chains, €800-1,200/m² for full renovation. (2) Pepe's Reformas — local, mid-range. (3) Habitissimo (habitissimo.es) — online platform to get quotes from multiple contractors. PERMITS: (1) Licencia de obra mayor — major renovation (structural changes). Apply at Madrid City Hall (Portal de Urbanismo). Takes 1-3 months. Fee €300-800. (2) Licencia de obra menor — minor work (painting, flooring). Takes 2-4 weeks. Fee €50-200. (3) Comunidad de Propietarios — you MUST notify your building's community before starting work. They can't refuse but may set conditions (hours, elevator use). COSTS: Full apartment renovation (80m²): €60,000-120,000. Kitchen: €8,000-20,000. Bathroom: €4,000-10,000. Flooring: €30-80/m². Painting: €10-20/m². TIMELINE: 2-4 months for full renovation (including permits). TIPS: (1) Get 3+ quotes. (2) Check the contractor's license ( Registro de Empresas Calificadas). (3) Never pay more than 30% upfront. (4) Get everything in writing. (5) For building permits, use a 'gestor' (administrative agent) — €200-400 to handle paperwork.

title: Meeting Rooms & Rehearsal Spaces
summary: Rent meeting rooms and rehearsal spaces by the hour. Spaces: WeWork, Atenasia, Espacio Ronda. €15-50/hour.
content: Meeting rooms and rehearsal spaces for rent in Madrid. MEETING ROOMS: (1) WeWork (3 locations: Velázquez, Caleido, Castellana) — meeting rooms for members and non-members. €15-30/hour. English. (2) Atenasia (Calle de la Zorrilla 7) — meeting rooms, videoconferencing. €20-40/hour. (3) Spaces (Calle de la Miguel Ángel 22) — Regus brand, meeting rooms €20-50/hour. (4) Regus (multiple) — business centers, meeting rooms €15-50/hour. (5) Impact Hub Madrid (Calle de la Goya 43) — coworking + meeting rooms, social enterprise focus. REHEARSAL SPACES (music/theater): (1) Espacio Ronda (Calle de la Ronda de Segovia 39) — rehearsal rooms for music, theater, dance. €10-15/hour. (2) La Nave (Calle de la Creta 12) — music rehearsal rooms. €8-12/hour. (3) Music Gallery (Calle de la Almirante 18) — band rehearsal, equipment included. €12-18/hour. (4) Estudio Río (Calle del Río Tajo 14) — recording + rehearsal. €15-25/hour. CONFERENCE ROOMS (larger events): (1) Hotel meeting rooms — most hotels rent meeting rooms. €200-800/day. (2) Círculo de Bellas Artes (Calle de Alcalá 42) — event spaces, €500-2,000/day. (3) Matadero Madrid (Plaza de Legazpi) — cultural event spaces. (4) IFEMA (Campo de las Naciones) — large conference venue, 10,000+ capacity. FREE OPTIONS: (1) Madrid public libraries have free meeting rooms (book ahead, 1 week). (2) Some cafés (Starbucks, Café de Oriente) allow small meetings with purchase. TIPS: (1) Book 1-2 weeks ahead for meeting rooms. (2) Bring your own laptop/HDMI adapter. (3) Most spaces have wifi and whiteboards. (4) For rehearsal spaces, bring your own instruments (some have drum kits/PAs). (5) Coworking spaces often offer 1 free meeting room hour per day for members.
